_comment: Per-chart narratives. CI (build_prompt.py -> GitHub Models -> write_narrative.py) overwrites these from live numbers; this committed copy is the fallback so the page always reads well. source = 'fallback' | 'github-models'.

notes.shape: The yield curve graphs Treasury yields across maturities, typically rising to reflect higher yields for longer durations, signaling normal economic conditions. An inverted curve, where shorter-term yields exceed longer-term yields, has historically signaled recessions with a lead time of 6-18 months. Currently, the 10-year Treasury yield stands at 4.57% (maturing June 2026), and the 10Y-2Y spread is 0.37 percentage points, indicating a positively sloped curve. This contrasts with the 2022-2024 period when the curve was inverted or flat, reaching a lowest inversion of 0.0 points, before recently re-steepening above zero.
notes.signal: The 2s10s spread (difference between 10-year and 2-year yields) serves as a recession indicator, with inversion (negative spread) historically preceding recessions. The yield curve was inverted throughout 2022-2024, signaling economic caution, but has since re-steepened to +0.37 points, implying a reduced immediate recession risk. The NY Fed recession probability stands at 31%, reflecting moderate recession risk but not certainty. It is important to note that the yield curve can un-invert shortly before an actual recession, so positive steepness does not eliminate recession possibilities.
notes.now: Currently, the yield curve's positive slope and moderate NY Fed recession probability indicate some easing of recession concerns compared to the inversion period. However, these signals should be considered alongside historical context and the caveat that un-inversion can precede recessions, suggesting cautious monitoring remains warranted.
